As mentioned before, python is a general purpose programming language.
What allowed python to become a favorite for data scientists, is the support of community developed libraries.
In this chapter, we will introduce a some of these libraries.

allows python to perform vectorized calculations and matrix operations.
It offers advanced features beyond the capabilities of built-in python lists and tuples.
It also provides a library of mathematical functions, random number generators, etc.
Compared to built-in functions, python is able to perform efficient calculations by leveraging C code.
NumPy relies on its own compound class called
(which stands for n-dimensional array, also referred to as

).
Here’s a simple one-dimensional array, created using the constructor
You can create multidimensional arrays by providing the constructor with a nested list of numbers:
class comes with useful attributes such as:
which represents the dimensions of the array
which represents the number of dimensions of the array
which represents the datatype of the array’s elements
which represents the number of elements in the array.
mat.dtype.name #use the dtype.name to show the string representation of the datatype
You can also create a NumPy array using other useful constructors:
) on NumPy arrays, the operations are treated as element wise operations.

You can use these operations on any pair of arrays with compatible shapes.
Shape compatibility in NumPy works with respect to the NumPy broadcasting rules.
NumPy first compares number of dimensions.
If the two operands have unequal dimensions, NumPy implicitly adds dimensions of size one to the left of the lower-dimensional operand.
For example, when attempting to perform operations on a pair of arrays with the shapes:

children.html: 3×4×53 \times 4 \times 53×4×5

children.value: , the second array is converted to a

children.html: 1×1×51 \times 1 \times 51×1×5

children.value:
With the number of dimensions settled, NumPy checks if each dimension has compatible sizes.
Sizes are compatible if they are equal to each other or one of them is equal to 1.
If a dimension is exactly one, the array is stretched to accommodate the larger array.

A = np.arange(1,61).reshape((3,4,5)) # reshape is a method used change the dimensions of the array
B = np.array([1,2,3,4,5])
has two dimensions, it is implicitly reshaped into a

children.html: 1×1×51 \times 1 \times 51×1×5

children.value:
array.
Based on the broadcasting rules

of compatible sizes.
To accommodate the elements of
are stretched to a goal shape of

children.html: 3×4×53 \times 4 \times 53×4×5

children.value:
.
First, it is stretched into a

children.html: 4×54 \times 54×5

children.value: array by copying itself four times, similar to the following operation:

twoDimensions = np.array([B] * 4) # stretches using list multiplication
twoDimensions
The resulting array is then stretched 3 times to reach the goal shape

children.html: 3×4×53 \times 4 \times 53×4×5

children.value:
With the sizes now matching, it applies the calculation.
You can compare that the calculations indeed match:

print("product from automatic broadcasting:")
print(A * B)
print("product from explicit stretching")
print(A * threeDimensions)
Here’s another example involving an array of size

children.html: 5×15 \times 15×1

children.value: To accommodate each other, both arrays are stretched into

children.html: 4×54 \times 54×5

children.value:
stretchedC = np.array([C] * 5)
stretchedD = np.repeat(D, 4, axis = 1) # stretches the column into 5 columns

print("product from automatic broadcasting")
print(C * D)
print("product from explicit stretching")
print(stretchedC * stretchedD)

children.value:
NumPy also features special mathematical functions that can be applied to scalar values or arrays.
When applied to arrays, these functions are applied element-wise.
These functions are known as universal functions (
). You can find a reference of universal functions
Arrays in NumPy also support advanced indexing including slicing, logical indexing, and iteration:

arr = np.arange(0,20)
print(arr[10:15])
print(arr[3:12:2])
print(arr[:5:-2])
print(arr[arr % 2 == 0])

library features is used for all manners of data analysis tasks.
It features new classes like

Behind the scenes it is also supported by
is a core data structure in
used to represent vectors of any datatype.
There are multiple ways to construct a

.
In the example below we create a series by passing a
array as an argument to the constructor.

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

pd.Series(np.arange(100,601,100))

objects come with indices.
By default, indices are set to

children.html: [0,1,2,⋯ ,n−1][0,1,2, \cdots, n-1][0,1,2,⋯,n−1]

children.value:
for a series with n elements.
Indices can be any datatype, as long as the number of elements in the series match the number of elements in index.
You can think of indices as the dictionary keys and the elements as the values.

As shown above, indices don’t need to be unique.
But in most cases you would want unique indices to avoid indexing issues.

supports vectorized functions, element-wise operations and advanced indexing.
Using these operations simply delegates the calculation to

instances.
When you do this, the function will be applied element-wise.
Pandas will try to coerce the
elements into numeric types when used on

s.
If the elements can’t be coerced, it will result into an error.

Note that when performing element-wise operations involving multiple series, elements are not matched by position.
Instead, matching indices are paired up and the operations are performed on each pair.

Such chained assignment never works to update the original DataFrame or Series, because the intermediate object on which we are setting values always behaves as a copy (due to Copy-on-Write).

Try using '.loc[row_indexer, col_indexer] = value' instead, to perform the assignment in a single step.

See the documentation for a more detailed explanation: https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/user_guide/copy_on_write.html#chained-assignment

children.value: You can learn more about advanced

is used to create highly customizable visualizations.
The library supports multiple subplot visualizations, customizable axes, tick-marks, tick labels, axes, markers, and more.
It also seamlessly works with
Here’s an example of a simple visualization of a plot containing one line plot.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
x_values = np.arange(-100, 100)
ax.plot(x_values, x_values ** 3)

plt.show()
In the example above, we initialize a visualization using the function

.
This function returns a pair (a tuple with two elements) of values, a figure object and an axes object.
These are unpacked and stored to the variables

a visualization is called a figure.
A figure can be made up of multiple subplots.
Since we initialized
(no arguments), by defaults, the generated figure has one subplot.
For example, if you want a 2x3 grid of subplots, then you need to initialize your subplots as such:

fig2, axs = plt.subplots(2,3)
axs[0][2].plot(x_values, x_values ** 3)
plt.show()
To edit a specific subplot, you simply need to index them by their row and column number in the grid.
You can learn more about the types of visualization you can create in
, how to customize a plot and more in their user guide
